revision.comment: [[三重県]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿にある神社。[[熊野権現]]がかつて降臨した地とされる。
revision.text:
'''貴禰谷神社'''（きねがだにじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿にある神社。紀宝町鵜殿地区の西のはずれにある丘陵中腹に、社名を刻銘した石碑が祀られた小祠があり、地元では権現谷・矢淵（やぶち）権現・石淵（いわぶち）権現などと呼ばれる<ref name="heibon_1997_221">平凡社[1997: 221]</ref>。
「熊野権現垂迹縁起」（『[[長寛勘文]]』所収）によれば、[[熊野権現]]は[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[伊予国|伊予]]・[[淡路国|淡路]]・[[紀伊国|紀伊]]などを遍歴した後、[[新宮市|新宮]]の[[神倉神社|神倉]]を経て、[[阿須賀神社|阿須賀社]]の北の石淵（いわぶち）の谷に結・速玉と家津美御子の二宇の社として祀られと<ref>宮家[1992: 2]</ref>され、ここで言う「北の石淵の谷」の跡地が貴禰谷神社であるという。これら3柱の祭神は、後に[[熊野本宮大社|熊野本宮]]と[[熊野速玉大社|熊野新宮]]の2社に遷し祀られた<ref name="heibon_1997_221"/>。
== 注 == {{Reflist}}
== 文献 == * 平凡社、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * 宮家 準、1992、『熊野修験』、吉川弘文館（日本歴史叢書） ISBN 4642066497
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野権現]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:きねかたにしんしや}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:紀宝町]]

'''貴禰谷神社'''（きねがだにじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿にある神社。紀宝町鵜殿地区の西のはずれにある丘陵中腹に、社名を刻銘した石碑が祀られた小祠があり、地元では'''権現谷'''・'''矢淵'''（やぶち）'''権現'''・'''石淵'''（いわぶち）'''権現'''などと呼ばれる<ref name="heibon_1997_221">平凡社[1997: 221]</ref>。
「熊野権現垂迹縁起」（『[[長寛勘文]]』所収）によれば、[[熊野権現]]は[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[伊予国|伊予]]・[[淡路国|淡路]]・[[紀伊国|紀伊]]などを遍歴した後、[[新宮市|新宮]]の[[神倉神社|神倉]]を経て、[[阿須賀神社|阿須賀社]]の北の石淵（いわぶち）の谷に結・速玉と家津美御子の二宇の社として祀られと<ref>宮家[1992: 2]</ref>され、ここで言う「北の石淵の谷」の跡地が貴禰谷神社であるという。これら3柱の祭神は、後に[[熊野本宮大社|熊野本宮]]と[[熊野速玉大社|熊野新宮]]の2社に遷し祀られた<ref name="heibon_1997_221"/>。
== 注 == {{Reflist}}
== 文献 == * 平凡社、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * 宮家 準、1992、『熊野修験』、吉川弘文館（日本歴史叢書） ISBN 4642066497
== 関連項目 == * [[熊野権現]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:きねかたにしんしや}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:紀宝町]]

revision.comment: 情報函設置、記述をやや改変。祭神・社格が不明なので憶測をｺﾒﾝﾄｱｳﾄしてみる
revision.text: {{神社 |名称 = 貴禰谷神社 |画像 = |所在地 = [[三重県]][[南牟婁郡]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|44|08|N|135|59|59|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = |社格 = |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = 王子造 |別名 = 矢淵権現・石淵権現 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''貴禰谷神社'''（きねがだにじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[南牟婁郡]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿にある神社。紀宝町鵜殿地区の西のはずれにある丘陵中腹に、社名を刻銘した石碑が祀られた小祠があり、地元では'''権現谷'''・'''矢淵'''（やぶち）'''権現'''・'''石淵'''（いわぶち）'''権現'''などと呼ばれる平凡社[1997: 221]。紀宝町指定の史跡。 == 祭神 == {{節stub}} == 由緒 == 『[[長寛勘文]]』所収の「熊野権現御垂迹縁起」によれば、[[熊野権現]]は[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[伊予国|伊予]]・[[淡路国|淡路]]・[[紀伊国|紀伊]]などを遍歴した後、[[新宮市|新宮]]の[[神倉神社|神倉]]を経て、[[阿須賀神社|阿須賀社]]の北の石淵（いわぶち）の谷に結・速玉と家津美御子の2宇の社として祀られとされるが、『[[熊野年代記]]』によれば[[安寧天皇]]18年に新宮神倉に[[垂迹]]した熊野三所権現を後に貴祢谷へ[[勧請]]したとあり、[[孝昭天皇]]5年に2宇の社壇を建立したとあって、「御垂迹縁起」に言う「北の石淵の谷」を「貴祢谷」としている。その跡地が当神社であるとされるが、これら3柱の祭神は、[[崇神天皇]]の時代に家津美御子神が[[熊野本宮大社|熊野本宮]]へ『[[水鏡]]』、『[[皇年代略記]]』、『[[帝王編年記]]』、『[[扶桑略記]]』等。、[[景行天皇]]の時代に結・速玉の神が[[熊野速玉大社|熊野新宮]]へ遷し祀られたと伝え『[[水鏡]]』、『[[帝王編年記]]』、『[[扶桑略記]]』、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』等。、鵜殿の伝承では、新宮大社への遷座に際して当地の住人が[[諸手船]]で先導したといい平凡社[1983] 、それに因んで現在も10月16日の速玉大社の[[御船祭]]で、鵜殿に鎮座する[[烏止野神社]]の氏子が諸手船を漕ぎ出し、神船を先導するという。 == 注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[熊野権現]] == 文献 == * 平凡社、1983、『三重県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系24）、平凡社 ISBN 9784582490244 * 平凡社、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * 宮家 準、1992、『熊野修験』、吉川弘文館（日本歴史叢書） ISBN 4642066497 == 外部リンク == *[http://www.town.kiho.mie.jp/tour/23/ 貴祢谷社]（紀宝町サイト） {{DEFAULTSORT:きねかたにしんしや}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:紀宝町]]

{{神社 |名称 = 貴禰谷神社 |画像 = |所在地 = [[三重県]][[南牟婁郡]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|44|08|N|135|59|59|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = |社格 = |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = 王子造 |別名 = 矢淵権現・石淵権現 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''貴禰谷神社'''（きねがだにじんじゃ）は[[三重県]][[南牟婁郡]][[紀宝町]]鵜殿にある神社。紀宝町鵜殿地区の西のはずれにある丘陵中腹に、社名を刻銘した石碑が祀られた小祠があり、地元では'''権現谷'''・'''矢淵'''（やぶち）'''権現'''・'''石淵'''（いわぶち）'''権現'''などと呼ばれる平凡社[1997: 221]。紀宝町指定の史跡。 == 祭神 == {{節stub}} == 由緒 == 『[[長寛勘文]]』所収の「熊野権現御垂迹縁起」によれば、[[熊野権現]]は[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[伊予国|伊予]]・[[淡路国|淡路]]・[[紀伊国|紀伊]]などを遍歴した後、[[新宮市|新宮]]の[[神倉神社|神倉]]を経て、[[阿須賀神社|阿須賀社]]の北の石淵（いわぶち）の谷に結・速玉と家津美御子の2宇の社として祀られとされるが、『[[熊野年代記]]』によれば[[安寧天皇]]18年に新宮神倉に[[垂迹]]した熊野三所権現を後に貴祢谷へ[[勧請]]したとあり、[[孝昭天皇]]5年に2宇の社壇を建立したとあって、「御垂迹縁起」に言う「北の石淵の谷」を「貴祢谷」としている。その跡地が当神社であるとされるが、これら3柱の祭神は、[[崇神天皇]]の時代に家津美御子神が[[熊野本宮大社|熊野本宮]]へ『[[水鏡]]』、『[[皇年代略記]]』、『[[帝王編年記]]』、『[[扶桑略記]]』等。、[[景行天皇]]の時代に結・速玉の神が[[熊野速玉大社|熊野新宮]]へ遷し祀られたと伝え『[[水鏡]]』、『[[帝王編年記]]』、『[[扶桑略記]]』、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』等。、鵜殿の伝承では、新宮大社への遷座に際して当地の住人が[[諸手船]]で先導したといい平凡社[1983] 、それに因んで現在も10月16日の速玉大社の[[熊野速玉祭#御船祭（10月16日）|御船祭]]で、鵜殿に鎮座する[[烏止野神社]]の氏子が諸手船を漕ぎ出し、神船を先導するという。 == 注 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[熊野権現]] == 文献 == * 平凡社、1983、『三重県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系24）、平凡社 ISBN 9784582490244 * 平凡社、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * 宮家 準、1992、『熊野修験』、吉川弘文館（日本歴史叢書） ISBN 4642066497 == 外部リンク == *[http://www.town.kiho.mie.jp/tour/23/ 貴祢谷社]（紀宝町サイト） {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:きねかたにしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:紀宝町の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=August 25, 2020}} {{Original research|date=August 25, 2020}} {{More footnotes|date=August 25, 2020}} {{Shrine infobox | Name = Kinegaya Shrine | image = {{image needed|cat=Minamimuro District}} | location = Udono, [[Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[Minamimuro District, Mie|Minamimuro District]], [[Mie Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|33|44|08|N|135|59|59|E|scale:25000|display=inline,title}} | Deities = | Rank = | Founded = Unknown | Main hall = Ōji-zukuri | Other Names = Gongen Valley, Yabuchi Gongen, Iwabuchi Gongen | Festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kinegaya Shrine''' (貴禰谷神社, Kinegaya Jinja) is a shrine located in Udono, [[Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. It is situated on the western edge of the Udono district on the middle slope of a hill. The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities == <!-- Possible deities include: * [[Izanami]] * [[Hayatamano Okami|Hayatama-no-Ōkami]] * [[Ketsumimiko no Okami|Ketsumimiko-no-Ōkami]] --> {{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through [[Buzen Province]], [[Iyo Province]], [[Awaji Province]], and [[Kii Province]] before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū]]. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related topics == * [[Kumano Gongen]]
== References == * Heibonsha, 1983, *"The Place Names of Mie Prefecture"* (日本歴史地名大系24), Heibonsha, ISBN 9784582490244 * Heibonsha, 1997, *"Comprehensive Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii"* (大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典), Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5 * Miyake, Jun (1992). *Kumano Shugendō* (熊野修験). Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, ISBN 4642066497
== External links == * [http://www.town.kiho.mie.jp/tour/23/ Kinegaya Shrine] (Official Kihō Town website)

The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities == <!-- Possible deities include: * [[Izanami]] * [[Hayatamano Okami|Hayatama-no-Ōkami]] * [[Ketsumimiko no Okami|Ketsumimiko-no-Ōkami]] --> {{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through [[Buzen Province]], [[Iyo Province]], [[Awaji Province]], and [[Kii Province]] before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū]]. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related topics == * [[Kumano Gongen]]
== References == * Heibonsha, 1983, *"The Place Names of Mie Prefecture"* (日本歴史地名大系24), Heibonsha, ISBN 9784582490244 * Heibonsha, 1997, *"Comprehensive Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii"* (大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典), Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5 * Miyake, Jun (1992). *Kumano Shugendō* (熊野修験). Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, ISBN 4642066497
== External links == * [http://www.town.kiho.mie.jp/tour/23/ Kinegaya Shrine] (Official Kihō Town website)

{{Citation needed|date=August 25, 2020}} {{Original research|date=August 25, 2020}} {{More footnotes|date=August 25, 2020}} {{Shrine infobox | Name = Kinegaya Shrine | image = {{image needed|cat=Minamimuro District}} | location = Udono, [[Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[Minamimuro District, Mie|Minamimuro District]], [[Mie Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|33|44|08|N|135|59|59|E|scale:25000|display=inline,title}} | Deities = | Rank = | Founded = Unknown | Main hall = Ōji-zukuri | Other Names = Gongen Valley, Yabuchi Gongen, Iwabuchi Gongen | Festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kinegaya Shrine''' (貴禰谷神社, Kinegaya Jinja) is a shrine located in Udono, [[Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[Mie Prefecture]]. It is situated on the western edge of the Udono district on the middle slope of a hill. The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities == <!-- Possible deities include: * [[Izanami]] * [[Hayatamano Okami|Hayatama-no-Ōkami]] * [[Ketsumimiko no Okami|Ketsumimiko-no-Ōkami]] --> {{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through [[Buzen Province]], [[Iyo Province]], [[Awaji Province]], and [[Kii Province]] before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in [[Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū]]. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== Related topics == * [[Kumano Gongen]]
== References == * Heibonsha, 1983, *"The Place Names of Mie Prefecture"* (日本歴史地名大系24), Heibonsha, ISBN 9784582490244 * Heibonsha, 1997, *"Comprehensive Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii"* (大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典), Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5 * Miyake, Jun (1992). *Kumano Shugendō* (熊野修験). Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, ISBN 4642066497
== External links == * [http://www.town.kiho.mie.jp/tour/23/ Kinegaya Shrine] (Official Kihō Town website)

From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>

'''Kinegaya Shrine''' (貴禰谷神社, Kinegaya Jinja) is a shrine located in Udono, [[:en:Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It is situated on the western edge of the Udono district on the middle slope of a hill. The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities ==
{{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]], [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]], [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]], and [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū]]. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== See Also == * [[Kumano Gongen]]
== References == * Heibonsha, 1983, *"The Place Names of Mie Prefecture"* (日本歴史地名大系24), Heibonsha, ISBN 9784582490244 * Heibonsha, 1997, *"Comprehensive Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii"* (大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典), Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5 * Miyake, Jun (1992). *Kumano Shugendō* (熊野修験). Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, ISBN 4642066497

{{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]], [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]], [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]], and [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū]]. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.

In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:貴禰谷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:貴禰谷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:貴禰谷神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11634797]] Wikidata name: Kinegadani-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:18, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:11, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=August 25, 2020}} {{Original research|date=August 25, 2020}} {{More footnotes|date=August 25, 2020}} {{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kinegaya Shrine | image = {{image needed|cat=Minamimuro District}} | location = Udono, [[:en:Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[:en:Minamimuro District, Mie|Minamimuro District]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{Coord|33.735556|N|135.999722|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = | Rank = | Founded = Unknown | Main hall = Ōji-zukuri | Other Names = Gongen Valley, Yabuchi Gongen, Iwabuchi Gongen | Festival = | Rituals = | prefecture= {{ill|Kiho|WD=Q1203117}} }}
'''Kinegaya Shrine''' (貴禰谷神社, Kinegaya Jinja) is a shrine located in Udono, [[:en:Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]]. It is situated on the western edge of the Udono district on the middle slope of a hill. The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities ==
{{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]], [[:en:Iyo Province|Iyo Province]], [[:en:Awaji Province|Awaji Province]], and [[:en:Kii Province|Kii Province]] before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in [[:en:Shingū, Wakayama|Shingū]]. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== See Also == * [[Kumano Gongen]]
== References == * Heibonsha, 1983, *"The Place Names of Mie Prefecture"* (日本歴史地名大系24), Heibonsha, ISBN 9784582490244 * Heibonsha, 1997, *"Comprehensive Dictionary of Temples and Shrines in Yamato and Kii"* (大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典), Heibonsha, ISBN 458213402-5 * Miyake, Jun (1992). *Kumano Shugendō* (熊野修験). Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, ISBN 4642066497
== External links == * [http://www.town.kiho.mie.jp/tour/23/ Kinegaya Shrine] (Official Kihō Town website)

Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>

'''Kinegaya Shrine''' (貴禰谷神社, Kinegaya Jinja) is a shrine located in Udono, [[:en:Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}. It is situated on the western edge of the Udono district on the middle slope of a hill. The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities ==
{{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through {{ill|Buzen Province|en|Buzen Province|WD=Q1047134|lt=Buzen Province}}, {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|WD=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province}}, {{ill|Awaji Province|en|Awaji Province|WD=Q1053717|lt=Awaji Province}}, and {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province}} before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū}}. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}

Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>

'''Kinegaya Shrine''' (貴禰谷神社, Kinegaya Jinja) is a shrine located in Udono, [[:en:Kihō, Mie|Kihō Town]], {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}. It is situated on the western edge of the Udono district on the middle slope of a hill. The shrine, marked by a stone monument with its name, is locally referred to as '''Gongen Valley''', '''Yabuchi Gongen''', or '''Iwabuchi Gongen'''.<ref name="heibon_1997_221">Heibonsha [1997: 221]</ref> It is a designated historic site of Kihō Town.
== Deities ==
{{Stub section}}
== History == According to the "Legend of the Manifestation of Kumano Gongen," recorded in the '''Chōkan Kambun''' (長寛勘文), the [[Kumano Gongen]] traveled through {{ill|Buzen Province|en|Buzen Province|WD=Q1047134|lt=Buzen Province|1=Buzen Province}}, {{ill|Iyo Province|en|Iyo Province|WD=Q907829|lt=Iyo Province|1=Iyo Province}}, {{ill|Awaji Province|en|Awaji Province|WD=Q1053717|lt=Awaji Province|1=Awaji Province}}, and {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|WD=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}} before being enshrined at [[Kamakura Shrine]] in {{ill|Shingū, Wakayama|en|Shingū, Wakayama|WD=Q823526|lt=Shingū|1=Shingū, Wakayama}}. From there, it was moved north to a valley known as '''Iwabuchi''' (Stone Pool), where two shrines—dedicated to [[Yui Okami]] (結大神) and [[Hayatama Okami]] (速玉大神)—were established.
The '''Kumano Chronicles''' (熊野年代記) state that in the 18th year of Emperor Annei’s reign, Kumano's three deities manifested at [[Kamikura Shrine]] and were later enshrined at this site, called Kinegaya. In the 5th year of Emperor Kōshō's reign, two shrines were built in this area. This site is believed to be the location of the current Kinegaya Shrine.
The three deities—[[Yui Okami]], [[Hayatama Okami]], and [[Ketsumimiko Okami]]—were eventually relocated to other shrines. Yui Okami and Hayatama Okami were moved to [[Kumano Hayatama Taisha]], while Ketsumimiko Okami was enshrined at [[Kumano Hongū Taisha|Kumano Hongu Taisha]].<ref>Sources include *Suikyō* (水鏡), *Kōdai Ryakuki* (皇代略記), and others.</ref>
Local lore suggests that during the relocation to Kumano Hayatama Taisha, residents of the area escorted the sacred palanquins in '''Morote-bune''' (double-handed boats). This tradition continues today in the '''Mifune Matsuri''' (Boat Festival) held on October 16, where the residents of Udono row boats to guide the sacred vessels.<ref>Heibonsha [1983]</ref>
